patient.name: Marcus Whelan
patient.chiefComplaint: Severe allergic reaction with airway swelling and shock after peanut exposure

history.hpi: 42-year-old male with known severe peanut allergy was eating dinner at a Thai restaurant about 12 minutes before EMS arrival. Within minutes of his first bite he developed itching of the lips and tongue, generalized hives, throat tightness, wheezing, and lightheadedness. His wife retrieved his EpiPen from his jacket and he self-administered one 0.3 mg IM dose in the lateral thigh approximately 8 minutes ago. He felt briefly better for 2-3 minutes, then rapidly deteriorated — stridor returned, he became diaphoretic, and he slid to the floor. Wife called 911. He is now obviously in severe anaphylaxis with shock physiology AND impending airway loss. The restaurant manager states the dish was prepared in a wok shared with peanut-containing dishes.
pastMedical: Severe IgE-mediated peanut allergy — two prior anaphylactic episodes (one ICU admission 4 years ago, intubated for 24h)
medications: EpiPen 0.3 mg auto-injector (one used 8 minutes ago)
history.lastOralIntake: Two bites of pad thai with chicken at ~1903 — approximately 12 minutes before EMS arrival. The wok was shared with peanut-containing dishes.
history.events: Acute IgE-mediated anaphylaxis to peanut at a restaurant. Onset within 2-3 minutes of first bite. Self-administered one EpiPen 0.3 mg IM about 8 minutes ago with transient improvement, then rapid biphasic-style decline despite that single epi dose. ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) on board may potentiate angioedema.

scene.dispatch: Medic 7, respond priority 1 to The Lotus Garden Restaurant, 2218 N. Halsted, for a 42-year-old male having a severe allergic reaction, can't breathe. RP is the wife, says he used his EpiPen and got worse. PD enroute for traffic control.
scene.location: Mid-size Thai restaurant, Friday dinner rush. Patient is on the floor between two booths near the back wall. Booths have been pushed apart; tablecloths and a half-eaten plate of pad thai are visible. Ambient noise high, dining room half-evacuated by staff.
scene.safety: Scene safe but cluttered. No ongoing threat. Aisle access narrowed by booths. Plan an early extrication path to the front door for cot loading. Restaurant staff are cooperative.
scene.bystanders: Wife (40s, on-scene, panicked but lucid) — confirms severe peanut allergy, two prior anaphylactic episodes (one ICU/intubation), single EpiPen 0.3 mg given approximately 8 minutes ago with brief improvement then rapid decline. She found him on the floor. Restaurant manager confirms the pad thai was prepared in a wok shared with peanut-containing dishes. Two off-duty servers ready to help carry equipment.
visualCues: Patient supine on the floor, mottled face, lips dusky and swollen
Diffuse urticarial rash across face, neck, chest, arms
Audible inspiratory stridor at the door
Used EpiPen auto-injector on the floor next to him (safety cap off, needle deployed)
Half-eaten plate of pad thai on the table; peanut garnish visible
Tongue and lip angioedema obvious from a distance
Wife clutching his coat — EpiPen prescription bottle visible in inside pocket
Restaurant manager directing other diners away from the scene

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: On arrival you find a 42-year-old male supine on the floor of a Thai restaurant in severe anaphylaxis. He is obtunded but rousable, with audible inspiratory stridor, expiratory wheeze, diffuse urticaria, lip/tongue angioedema, and obvious shock physiology (mottled, diaphoretic, weak radial pulse). His wife reports he used one EpiPen 0.3 mg IM about 8 minutes ago with brief improvement followed by rapid decline. He is on lisinopril (ACE-I) which may worsen angioedema. This patient has airway compromise + respiratory compromise + distributive/hypovolemic shock simultaneously — a high-mortality anaphylaxis presentation already failing a single dose of IM epi.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Threatened. Audible inspiratory stridor without a stethoscope. Tongue and lower lip visibly swollen — angioedema. Saliva pooling, no active vomiting. No foreign body. Voice muffled and hoarse — 'hot potato' voice.
clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 30, labored. Bilateral expiratory wheeze throughout all lung fields, inspiratory stridor at the upper airway. Marked intercostal and suprasternal retractions. Patient can only manage 2-3 word phrases.
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Radial pulse rapid, thready, ~138. Carotid present but weak. Capillary refill 4 seconds at fingertips. Skin warm/flushed at the trunk, mottled and pale at the extremities — classic distributive picture.

skin.color: Flushed trunk with diffuse urticaria; mottled extremities; perioral dusky tinge

clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Moves all 4 extremities purposefully but slowly. Hand to throat repeatedly.
otherFindings: Diffuse confluent urticaria across face, neck, chest, arms
Tongue and lower lip angioedema, hoarse muffled voice
Audible inspiratory stridor — UPPER airway involvement
Audible expiratory wheeze — LOWER airway involvement
Used EpiPen on the floor — one dose already given ~8 minutes ago
ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) on board — may worsen angioedema and complicate response

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize severe anaphylaxis (skin + airway + shock); call code resource early
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Anaphylaxis – More severe and is characterized by an acute onset involving: The skin (urticaria) and/or mucosa with either respiratory compromise or decreased BP or signs of end-organ dysfunction OR Hypotension for that patient after exposure to a known allergen OR Two or more of the following occurring rapidly after exposure to a likely allergen: Skin and/or mucosal involvement; Respiratory compromise (dyspnea, wheezing, stridor, hypoxemia); Persistent gastrointestinal symptoms; Hypotension or associated symptoms.
expectedActions.rationale: All three anaphylaxis pillars are present: skin, airway/respiratory, shock. Already failing one dose of IM epi. This will become a cardiac arrest if every minute is not aggressive. Identify it out loud and request additional resources NOW.

expectedActions.shortAction: Epinephrine 0.3 mg IM (1 mg/mL) anterolateral thigh — repeat dose #1 by EMS now
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Epinephrine administration is the primary treatment for anaphylaxis. If signs of anaphylaxis, administer epinephrine 1 mg/mL at the following dose and route: Adult (25 kg or more) 0.3 mg IM in the anterolateral thigh. If signs of anaphylaxis and hypoperfusion persist following the first dose of epinephrine, additional IM epinephrine can be repeated q5–15 minutes at above noted doses.
expectedActions.rationale: Patient gave himself one dose 8 minutes ago and is now worse. The MATC protocol explicitly authorizes repeat IM epi q5-15 min for persistent signs. Do not wait — this is the single highest-yield intervention.

expectedActions.shortAction: NRB 15 LPM, cardiac monitor, SpO2 + waveform EtCO2, position upright if tolerated
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Administer oxygen if needed for air hunger or respiratory distress and titrate to a target SPO2 of 94–98%. Depending on patient presentation, this may be accomplished with nasal cannula, nonrebreather, BVM, NIV. Continuous waveform capnography monitoring should be placed in line. Patients with significant respiratory distress should have continuous pulse oximetry and waveform capnography monitoring.
expectedActions.rationale: Hypoxia (86%) and stridor demand immediate high-flow O2. Cardiac monitor required — multiple epi doses + risk of arrest. EtCO2 trends will be your first sign of pending arrest. Keep him upright as long as airway is patent — supine worsens edema.

expectedActions.shortAction: Two large-bore IVs (16-18g) or IO if no IV in 90 sec
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Establish IV access. If unable to obtain within two attempts or less than 90 seconds, place an IO needle.
expectedActions.rationale: Anaphylactic shock will need MASSIVE volume + possibly an epi infusion + diphenhydramine + steroid. One IV is not enough. Two large-bore lines now while veins are still findable.

expectedActions.shortAction: NS 1 L wide open / pressure bag (90 kg × 20 mL/kg = 1800 mL — start first L now)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For signs of hypoperfusion, also administer 20 mL/kg isotonic fluid (normal saline or lactated Ringer's) rapidly (over 15 minutes) via IV or IO, and repeat as needed for ongoing hypoperfusion.
expectedActions.rationale: Distributive + hypovolemic shock. Anaphylaxis dumps intravascular volume into the tissues. Epi without volume often fails. 20 mL/kg is the protocol per-bolus dose; this patient will need multiple boluses.

expectedActions.shortAction: Nebulized epinephrine 1 mg/mL 5 mL for stridor
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If stridor is present, consider administering epinephrine 1 mg/mL, 5 mL nebulized.
expectedActions.rationale: Upper airway edema with stridor. Nebulized epi shrinks the supraglottic mucosa and buys time while IM/IV epi and fluids do their work. Indication is met directly per protocol.

expectedActions.shortAction: Albuterol 5 mg nebulized for wheezing (in-line with epi or back-to-back)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If respiratory distress with wheezing is present, consider administering Albuterol 2.5–5 mg nebulized AND/OR Epinephrine 1 mg/mL, 5 mL nebulized.
expectedActions.rationale: Lower-airway bronchospasm is treated with albuterol; upper-airway edema treated with nebulized epi. He has both — give both.

expectedActions.shortAction: Pre-brief difficult airway plan (edema); set up RSI + bougie + smaller ETT + cric kit
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Signs of a difficult airway (short jaw or limited jaw thrust or mobility, small thyromental space, upper airway obstruction, large tongue, obesity, large tonsils, large neck, craniofacial abnormalities, excessive facial hair, tracheostomy scar or evidence of other neck/facial surgery, trismus). Optimize patient for first-pass success with pre-procedure resuscitation, preoxygenation, positioning, sedatives and paralytics as indicated by patient presentation. When patients cannot be oxygenated/ventilated effectively using the above interventions, or when conventional airway approaches are impossible, surgical airway management is a reasonable option.
expectedActions.rationale: Tongue/lip angioedema + stridor = anatomically distorted upper airway. Plan for failure: video laryngoscopy, bougie, ETT one size smaller (6.5 or 7.0), and a surgical cricothyrotomy kit at the head BEFORE you need it.

expectedActions.shortAction: Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV (90 kg × 1 mg/kg = 90 mg → cap at 50 mg max) + Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Consider Methylprednisolone IV push or IM, 80-125 mg OR Dexamethasone IV push, IM, or PO, 8-16 mg. For urticaria or pruritus, administer a diphenhydramine 1 mg/kg, up to maximum dose of 50 mg IM, IV, or PO. The IV route is preferred for the patient in severe symptoms.
expectedActions.rationale: Adjuncts — neither will save him in the next 5 minutes, but both reduce relapse risk and skin/GI symptoms. IV route because he is critically ill. Do AFTER epi/fluids/airway are running, not before.

transitions.if: Repeat IM epi + fluids given, still hypotensive
transitions.condition: Crew gives second IM epi 0.3 mg, dual large-bore IVs, first L NS wide open, nebulized epi for stridor, and prepares difficult airway. After ~5 minutes the patient remains profoundly hypotensive (BP 60/palp), still stridorous, mental status drifting. Per protocol this is refractory anaphylaxis and requires IV/IO epinephrine push-dose or infusion plus continued aggressive volume.

transitions.if: Single IM epi only, no repeat at 5 min
transitions.condition: Crew gives one IM epi 0.3 mg and stops there. They focus on monitor placement and IV access but do not redose IM epi at 5 minutes, do not start the epi drip, do not give nebulized epi. They give a 250 mL bolus and stop. Over the next 4-5 minutes the patient continues to decline and arrests under worse conditions — no functioning IV running, no nebulized epi on board, and still on the floor.

transitions.if: Epi repeats given but only 250 mL fluid
transitions.condition: Crew correctly repeats IM epi and starts the epi drip, but limits fluid to a single 250 mL bolus 'because his lungs are wheezy' (misinterpreting bronchospasm as pulmonary edema). With profound vasodilation and capillary leak the patient arrests from hypovolemia + distributive collapse. Resuscitation is harder because the tank is empty.

phases.name: Refractory anaphylaxis
phases.description: Approximately 5 minutes after the EMS-delivered second IM epi, the patient remains in profound shock. BP is 60/palp despite the second IM epi and 1 L NS. Stridor is somewhat improved with nebulized epi but mental status is now drifting (GCS 10 → 8). Skin is more mottled. EtCO2 has fallen from 32 to 24 mmHg — falling cardiac output. This meets the MATC criterion for cardiovascular collapse despite IM epi and is the indication for an epinephrine IV infusion (0.05-0.5 mcg/kg/min) plus continued aggressive volume.
phases.triggerCondition: Five minutes after the EMS-administered repeat IM epinephrine, two large-bore IVs, first liter of NS, nebulized epi, and difficult-airway prep, the patient remains in cardiovascular collapse — BP 60/palp, GCS dropping, EtCO2 24, mottling progressing. Per MATC this is the indication for an epinephrine IV infusion plus continued isotonic fluid resuscitation toward 60 mL/kg.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Stridor noticeably softer after nebulized epi but tongue still swollen; voice muffled; patient now too obtunded to protect airway reliably.
clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 26 shallow. Wheeze still present but air movement reduced. Accessory muscle use easing as patient fatigues — not a good sign.
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Radial absent. Carotid weak at ~145. Capillary refill >5 seconds. Mottling extending centrally to chest.

skin.color: Mottled extending centrally; persistent urticaria; central cyanosis at lips

clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Withdraws to noxious; no purposeful movement; arm goes limp when released.
otherFindings: Cardiovascular collapse despite two IM epi doses + 1 L NS = REFRACTORY anaphylaxis
EtCO2 falling — failing cardiac output
GCS deteriorating — perfusion failure and CNS hypoxia
Airway will need to be definitively secured very soon

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize cardiovascular collapse refractory to IM epi — escalate to IV epi
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Consider an epinephrine IV drip (0.5 mcg/kg/minute) when cardiovascular collapse (hypotension with altered mental status, pallor, diaphoresis and/or delayed capillary refill) is present despite repeated IM doses of epinephrine in conjunction with at least 60 mL/kg isotonic fluid boluses.
expectedActions.rationale: The MATC criterion is met directly: persistent hypotension + AMS + pallor + delayed cap refill despite repeated IM epi. Switching to IV epi (push-dose or drip) is the next protocol step.

expectedActions.shortAction: Push-dose epi 10 mcg IV q3-5 min (dilute 1 mL of 0.1 mg/mL into 9 mL NS = 10 mcg/mL; give 1 mL)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Epinephrine, 0.05–0.3 mcg/kg/minute. Alternative to a drip, push dose epinephrine may be administered: Prepare 10 mcg/mL by diluting 1 mL of epinephrine 0.1 mg/mL (1:10,000) in 9 mL of normal saline. Administer 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 mL/kg) up to a maximum single dose of 10 mcg (1 mL) q 3–5 minutes titrated to maintain goal MAP.
expectedActions.rationale: Push-dose is the fastest bridge to the drip while the drip is being prepared. Calculation: 90 kg × 0.01 mg/kg from dilute 10 mcg/mL = capped at 10 mcg per dose per MATC. Titrate to MAP 65.

expectedActions.shortAction: Epinephrine infusion 0.1 mcg/kg/min (90 kg × 0.1 = 9 mcg/min); titrate 0.05-0.5 mcg/kg/min to MAP 65
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Consider an epinephrine IV drip (0.5 mcg/kg/minute) when cardiovascular collapse is present despite repeated IM doses of epinephrine in conjunction with at least 60 mL/kg isotonic fluid boluses. Epinephrine, 0.05–0.3 mcg/kg/minute.
expectedActions.rationale: Continuous epi is the definitive vasopressor for refractory anaphylactic shock. Start mid-range and titrate. The MATC anaphylaxis protocol references 0.5 mcg/kg/min; the shock protocol gives the safer titratable range of 0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min. Start low and go up — both are protocol.

expectedActions.shortAction: NS second liter wide open through second IV (toward 60 mL/kg = 5400 mL total goal)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For signs of hypoperfusion, also administer 20 mL/kg isotonic fluid (normal saline or lactated Ringer's) rapidly (over 15 minutes) via IV or IO, and repeat as needed for ongoing hypoperfusion. Consider an epinephrine IV drip when cardiovascular collapse is present despite repeated IM doses of epinephrine in conjunction with at least 60 mL/kg isotonic fluid boluses.
expectedActions.rationale: Anaphylactic shock is a tank-and-tone problem. Without volume, epi alone fails. MATC anchors the IV epi indication to '60 mL/kg isotonic fluid' — this patient needs roughly 5.4 L total. Be running the second liter through the second line right now.

expectedActions.shortAction: Third IM epi 0.3 mg if drip not yet running (q5-15 min interval)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If signs of anaphylaxis and hypoperfusion persist following the first dose of epinephrine, additional IM epinephrine can be repeated q5–15 minutes at above noted doses.
expectedActions.rationale: Don't let epinephrine stop. While the drip is being mixed, another IM epi at the 5-minute interval keeps a serum level. Stop IM epi once the infusion is reliably running.

expectedActions.shortAction: Move to RSI: pre-O2 BVM, ketamine 90 mg + rocuronium 90 mg IV ready; bougie + 7.0 ETT + cric kit at head
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Rapid/Delayed Sequence Intubation — Patient should be pre-oxygenated with high flow oxygen, and positive pressure ventilation for a minimum of two minutes prior to the procedure. Administer induction sedative agent: Etomidate IV push, 0.2-0.3 mg/kg OR Ketamine IV push, 1-2 mg/kg OR Midazolam IV push, 0.05-0.10 mg/kg. Administer paralytic agent: Succinylcholine IV push, 1-2 mg/kg OR Rocuronium IV push, 1 mg/kg OR Vecuronium IV push, 0.1 mg/kg.
expectedActions.rationale: GCS is now too low to protect the airway and the angioedema is getting worse, not better. Ketamine 1 mg/kg = 90 mg (sympathomimetic — preserves BP in shock). Rocuronium 1 mg/kg = 90 mg. Use bougie and smaller ETT (7.0). Pre-O2 BVM at 30° head-up while the drugs are drawn. Surgical airway kit visible on the floor next to you.

expectedActions.shortAction: Begin extrication to ambulance with ongoing resus; pre-alert closest ED with anaphylaxis-arrest pending
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Transport as soon as possible, and perform ongoing assessment as indicated. Cardiac monitoring is not required, but should be considered for those with known heart problems or who received multiple doses of epinephrine.
expectedActions.rationale: Refractory anaphylactic shock is a load-and-go condition once the lines, epi, and airway plan are in motion. ED needs lead time for a surgical airway kit, blood, and a code team.

transitions.if: Despite IV epi + fluids, progresses to PEA arrest
transitions.condition: Despite push-dose epi, the epi infusion ramping up, and the second liter of NS running, the patient loses pulses. Monitor shows a narrow-complex rhythm at ~130 with no palpable carotid — pulseless electrical activity. This is the classic anaphylactic arrest pattern: hypovolemic + distributive + obstructive (airway) physiology converging. Crew transitions to ACLS while keeping every anaphylaxis-specific intervention in play.

phases.name: PEA arrest
phases.description: Pulses lost. Monitor shows narrow-complex rhythm at ~130 with no carotid — pulseless electrical activity. This is an anaphylactic arrest: hypovolemic + distributive + obstructive (airway edema) all at once. The protocol playbook for non-shockable arrest applies (compressions + early epi), but anaphylaxis-specific interventions continue in parallel: massive fluid resuscitation, definitive airway despite edema, and anaphylaxis-cause-directed therapy. The H's and T's reversible-cause list applies — Hypovolemia and Hypoxia are the primary culprits here.
phases.triggerCondition: Patient loses pulses during refractory anaphylaxis. Monitor shows organized narrow-complex rhythm at HR ~130 with no palpable carotid pulse — PEA. Crew initiates ACLS while continuing epinephrine, massive volume, and definitive airway management with anaphylaxis-specific framing.

clinicalPresentation.airway: No spontaneous airway maintenance. Tongue and supraglottic edema present. Manual airway opening required. Plan: BVM with PEEP while compressions ongoing, then advanced airway between drug rounds.
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Apneic. No spontaneous chest rise. BVM with two-person technique, PEEP 5 cmH2O, in-line oxygen 15 LPM.
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Pulseless. Monitor shows organized rhythm — PEA. CPR in progress with feedback device, target depth 2-2.4 inches, rate 100-120, full recoil.

skin.color: Persistent urticaria; central cyanosis; mottling generalized

otherFindings: PEA — non-shockable; the rhythm is FINE, the heart is empty
Reversible cause is identified: anaphylaxis (Hypovolemia + Hypoxia + airway obstruction)
All anaphylaxis-specific interventions continue: epi infusion, massive NS, definitive airway
EtCO2 during compressions = primary feedback for CPR quality and ROSC detection

expectedActions.shortAction: High-quality CPR: rate 100-120, depth 2-2.4 in, full recoil, switch q2 min
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Effective chest compressions are defined as: A rate of greater than 100 and less than 120 compressions/minute; Depth of at least 2 inches (5 cm) and less than 2.4 inches (6 cm) for adults and children; Allow for complete chest recoil (avoid leaning); Minimize interruptions in compressions; Avoid rescuer fatigue by rotating rescuers at least every 2 minutes.
expectedActions.rationale: Compressions are the only thing moving the empty heart. Use a feedback device or metronome. Switch compressors every 2 minutes to prevent fatigue-related shallow depth.

expectedActions.shortAction: Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO push immediately (non-shockable rhythm = priority); repeat q3-5 min
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If feasible, IV or IO access should be obtained. Administer Epinephrine 1mg during the first or second round of compressions. Prioritize early administration of epinephrine for non-shockable rhythms.
expectedActions.rationale: Non-shockable rhythm: epi is the front-line drug and the protocol explicitly says prioritize early. Continue every 3-5 min. The IV epi INFUSION continues alongside the bolus doses — this is unusual but appropriate in anaphylactic arrest.

expectedActions.shortAction: MASSIVE NS — 2 L wide open through both IVs (continue toward 4-6 L total for anaphylaxis arrest)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Consider reversible causes of cardiac arrest which include the following: Hypovolemia. Additions to care include normal saline 2 L IV (or 20 mL/kg, repeated up to 3 times for pediatrics).
expectedActions.rationale: Hypovolemia from capillary leak is THE primary mechanism of this arrest. The cardiac arrest protocol specifies 2 L NS for hypovolemic arrest; anaphylaxis arrest typically needs 4-6 L (60 mL/kg = 5.4 L baseline before considering more). Run both lines wide open with pressure bags.

expectedActions.shortAction: Advanced airway: RSI / supraglottic if edema blocks ETT; bougie + 7.0 cuffed tube, surgical airway ready
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Advanced airway placement: Either a supraglottic airway or an endotracheal tube may be placed without interruption of compressions; Ventilations are provided at 10 breaths/minute for adults. When patients cannot be oxygenated/ventilated effectively using the above interventions, or when conventional airway approaches are impossible, surgical airway management is a reasonable option if the clinician has competency in the procedure and risk of death for not escalating airway management seems to outweigh the risk of a procedural complication.
expectedActions.rationale: Definitive airway during compressions. Anaphylaxis arrest airway is high-difficulty: tongue/supraglottic edema, prior trial of nebulized epi, possibly some response. Use video laryngoscopy + bougie; downsize ETT to 7.0; SGA as backup; surgical cricothyrotomy if neither works.

expectedActions.shortAction: Continue epi infusion alongside ACLS bolus epi (anaphylaxis-specific)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Consider an epinephrine IV drip (0.5 mcg/kg/minute) when cardiovascular collapse is present despite repeated IM doses of epinephrine in conjunction with at least 60 mL/kg isotonic fluid boluses.
expectedActions.rationale: Anaphylactic arrest is unusual because the BACKGROUND infusion makes sense — vasodilation is part of the etiology, not just a complication. ACLS bolus epi is layered on top, NOT a replacement.

expectedActions.shortAction: Waveform EtCO2 on advanced airway: target >10 during CPR; abrupt rise = ROSC
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Quantitative end-tidal capnography (EtCO2) should be used to monitor effectiveness of chest compressions. If EtCO2 less than 10 mmHg during the initial phases of resuscitation, attempt to improve chest compression quality. To monitor quality of CPR; as an early indicator of ROSC (rapid increase of 10–15 in EtCO2); to assist in evaluating prognosis for survival.
expectedActions.rationale: EtCO2 is the only real-time feedback for the chest. EtCO2 jumping from ~18 to 35+ is your ROSC indicator — call a pulse check at that moment, not during the next scheduled rhythm check.

expectedActions.shortAction: H's & T's: confirm Hypovolemia + Hypoxia drive (anaphylaxis); continue cause-directed therapy
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Consider reversible causes of cardiac arrest which include the following: Hypothermia; The dialysis patient/known hyperkalemic patient; Tricyclic antidepressant overdose; Hypovolemia; If the patient is intubated at the time of arrest, assess for tension pneumothorax and misplaced ETT.
expectedActions.rationale: Explicit cause-list review is the way the team avoids tunnel vision. In this case Hypovolemia + Hypoxia + airway obstruction are the drivers — every action is already aimed at them, but say it out loud.

expectedActions.shortAction: Consider glucagon 1-5 mg IV if on beta-blocker and refractory to epi (not this patient — note it)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Care Goals: Provide timely therapy for potentially life-threatening reactions to known or suspected allergens to prevent cardiorespiratory collapse and shock. Key Considerations: When anaphylaxis is suspected, EMS personnel should always consider epinephrine as first-line treatment.
expectedActions.rationale: Glucagon is not in the MATC anaphylaxis protocol as a routine step, but is reasonable clinical adjunct teaching for beta-blocked patients refractory to epi. This patient is on lisinopril (ACE-I), NOT a beta-blocker — name the consideration but do not give it. Teaching beat only.

transitions.if: ROSC achieved after epi + fluids + airway
transitions.condition: After 2-3 rounds of CPR with sustained epi infusion, scheduled 1 mg IV epi pushes, ~3 L NS administered, and a definitive airway placed with bougie-assisted 7.0 ETT, EtCO2 jumps from 18 to 38 and a carotid pulse returns. Rhythm is now sinus tachycardia at 118. BP 86/54. Transition to post-ROSC care with anaphylaxis-aware framing.

phases.name: ROSC — post-arrest care
phases.description: ROSC achieved. Sinus tachycardia at 118, BP 86/54 (still hypotensive but perfusing), EtCO2 28 on the ETT with continued BVM ventilation, GCS 4 (E1V1M2 — comatose, intubated). The patient remains in distributive shock physiology — vasodilation continues for hours after the trigger. Epi infusion continues to maintain MAP, volume continues, and ventilation is carefully titrated to avoid hyperventilation. Re-arrest risk is high. Destination should be a hospital capable of ICU admission and definitive airway management.
phases.triggerCondition: Carotid pulse returns and a perfusing organized rhythm is sustained at sinus tachycardia 118 with BP 86/54. EtCO2 jumped to 38 during the round signaling ROSC, now stabilizing in the high 20s on controlled ventilation. Crew transitions to post-ROSC management while continuing every anaphylaxis-specific therapy (epi infusion, volume, secured airway, steroids on board, antihistamine on board).

clinicalPresentation.airway: Endotracheal tube (7.0) at 22 cm at lip, capnography in line, bilateral chest rise, equal breath sounds, no epigastric sounds. Tube secured with commercial holder.
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Apneic without ventilator; receiving BVM at 10 breaths/min with PEEP 5 cmH2O. Bilateral wheeze still audible. Chest rise equal.
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Carotid palpable, radial weak. Skin still mottled but central pink returning. Capillary refill 4 sec.

skin.color: Persistent urticaria; central pink returning; peripheral mottling improving

clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Extension to noxious only — comatose post-arrest.
otherFindings: ROSC sustained — high re-arrest risk in the first 10-20 minutes
Distributive physiology continues — keep epi infusion running, keep volume running
EtCO2 28 — slightly low, target 35-45 with controlled rate
12-lead EKG required to screen for STEMI or arrest-related ischemia

expectedActions.shortAction: Re-confirm ETT with waveform EtCO2 + bilateral breath sounds; secure to 22 cm at lip
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Confirm placement of advanced airway (endotracheal tube, SGA) with waveform capnography (most reliable), absent gastric sounds, and bilateral breath sounds. Continuously monitor correct airway placement with waveform capnography during treatment and transport, paying particular attention to reassessing after each patient movement.
expectedActions.rationale: Compressions, patient movement, and CPR-driven thoracic shifts all displace tubes. Re-confirm now and again after every move (floor → cot → ambulance).

expectedActions.shortAction: Ventilate 10/min, target EtCO2 35-45; do NOT hyperventilate
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Do not hyperventilate. Maintain a ventilation rate of 8–10 breaths per minute, targeting an EtCO2 of 35–45 mmHg.
expectedActions.rationale: Hyperventilation is the most preventable cause of re-arrest. Lock the rate at 10/min, watch the waveform, titrate by EtCO2 — not by 'how it feels.'
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

expectedActions.shortAction: Titrate FiO2 to SpO2 94-98%; do NOT hyperoxygenate
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Administer oxygen as appropriate with a target of achieving 94–98% saturation. Do not hyperoxygenate.
expectedActions.rationale: Coming off 100% during the arrest, dial back to maintain SpO2 94-98%. Reactive oxygen species worsen neuro outcome.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

expectedActions.shortAction: Continue epi infusion + volume to maintain MAP ≥65; expect ongoing distributive shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For hypotension (SBP less than 90 mmHg or MAP less than 65 in adults) see Shock Guideline. Many patients experience 'stunning' of the cardiac muscle after ROSC. Hypotension is common, and volume resuscitation or vasopressor support is often required.
expectedActions.rationale: BP 86/54 = MAP 65 — at the floor. The drip is what got you ROSC; do not turn it off. Continue volume; expect to need 4-6 L total in a 90 kg adult anaphylactic.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

expectedActions.shortAction: 12-lead EKG; screen for STEMI or arrest-induced ischemia
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Perform serial 12-lead EKGs to assess for evidence of reversible cause of arrest such as STEMI or electrolyte derangement (e.g., hyperkalemia). Post-cardiac arrest patients with evidence or interpretation consistent with ST elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI/acute MI) should be transported preferably to a facility capable of emergent cardiac catheterization.
expectedActions.rationale: Anaphylactic arrest can unmask demand ischemia or trigger Kounis syndrome (allergic coronary spasm). Get the 12-lead now and at 10 min into transport.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

expectedActions.shortAction: Continuous monitor for re-arrest; pads on; epi/fluids running; mechanical CPR device ready
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Monitor closely for recurrence of cardiac arrest using clinical and adjunctive criteria such as cardiac monitoring, EtCO2 monitoring, and physical signs of perfusion. The condition of post-resuscitation patients fluctuates rapidly and continuously requiring close monitoring. A significant percentage of post-ROSC patients will re-arrest. Maintain mechanical CPR device in place in preparation for re-arrest.
expectedActions.rationale: Re-arrest in the first 10-20 min is common in anaphylactic shock. Keep pads on, keep the epi running, keep the CPR feedback gear staged.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

expectedActions.shortAction: Ketamine 50 mg IV q15 min for sedation (90 kg × 0.5-1 mg/kg = 45-90 mg)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Intubated patients should be provided appropriate sedation with sedative or opioid medications, and sedation titrated to an appropriate target level using RASS score or similar scale. Consider sedation with sedative and/or opioid medications if agitated: Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg And Midazolam 0.02 mg/kg OR Ketamine IV push, 0.5-1 mg/kg.
expectedActions.rationale: Comatose but intubated — sedation needed once perfusion is restored. Ketamine preserves BP better than midazolam in this still-hypotensive patient.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport to closest STEMI/ICU-capable receiving center; pre-alert ED for post-arrest anaphylaxis
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Post-cardiac arrest patients with evidence or interpretation consistent with ST elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI/acute MI) should be transported preferably to a facility capable of emergent cardiac catheterization or, as a secondary option, to a STEMI receiving facility based upon local resources and system of care. Consider transporting patients to an age-appropriate facility which offers specialized adult or pediatric post-resuscitation care.
expectedActions.rationale: ICU, cath lab access, and a code team ready at the door. Give the ED 5+ minutes lead time so a vent, A-line, and pressors are ready.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

phases.name: One-and-done IM epi
phases.description: JUDGMENT-ERROR BRANCH #1. The crew administers one IM epi 0.3 mg shortly after arrival and stops there. They focus on monitor application and a single IV but do not redose IM epi at 5 minutes, do not give nebulized epi for stridor, do not start a drip, and give only a 250 mL bolus. Approximately 4-5 minutes later the patient arrests in PEA — under far worse conditions than the default path. The recovery requires recognizing the omission, restarting aggressive epi (both IM repeat AND IV push-dose), opening a second large-bore line for massive volume, and securing a now-more-edematous airway.
phases.triggerCondition: After the single EMS-given IM epi 0.3 mg, no further epinephrine is administered for 5+ minutes despite persistent anaphylaxis and shock. Nebulized epi is not given. The patient deteriorates and arrests in PEA. The crew must recognize the miss, escalate aggressively, and resuscitate under worse conditions than the default path (no drip yet, only one IV, minimal fluid in).

clinicalPresentation.airway: No tone. Tongue/lip angioedema progressed. Stridor was never treated with nebulized epi. Manual jaw thrust required; suction at the ready.

skin.color: Generalized mottling, central cyanosis, persistent urticaria

otherFindings: Single IM epi given, never repeated — protocol violation
No nebulized epi for stridor — protocol violation
Only 250 mL fluid given — protocol under-resuscitation
Airway is harder now because edema progressed unchecked

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize the miss: single IM epi was inadequate — RESCUE NOW
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If signs of anaphylaxis and hypoperfusion persist following the first dose of epinephrine, additional IM epinephrine can be repeated q5–15 minutes at above noted doses. Consider an epinephrine IV drip (0.5 mcg/kg/minute) when cardiovascular collapse is present despite repeated IM doses of epinephrine in conjunction with at least 60 mL/kg isotonic fluid boluses.
expectedActions.rationale: Naming the omission is the leadership move. The crew must hit the reset and execute every step that was skipped — now during compressions.

expectedActions.shortAction: High-quality CPR + airway management; epi 1 mg IV push immediately
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Initiate chest compressions in cases with no bystander chest compressions. If feasible, IV or IO access should be obtained. Administer Epinephrine 1mg during the first or second round of compressions. Prioritize early administration of epinephrine for non-shockable rhythms.
expectedActions.rationale: Standard non-shockable arrest steps — but recognize the patient is volume-depleted from inadequate fluids AND has an edematous airway from undertreated anaphylaxis.

expectedActions.shortAction: NS 2 L wide open through both IVs (place IO if second IV impossible)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Hypovolemia. Additions to care include normal saline 2 L IV (or 20 mL/kg, repeated up to 3 times for pediatrics). If unable to obtain within two attempts or less than 90 seconds, place an IO needle.
expectedActions.rationale: Only 250 mL of fluid is in. The patient is empty. Pressurize the bag, run both lines, place humeral IO if access is poor — anaphylactic arrest needs 4-6 L total.

expectedActions.shortAction: Definitive airway under harder conditions: BVM with PEEP, video laryngoscopy + bougie + 7.0 ETT, surgical airway ready
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Optimize patient for first-pass success with pre-procedure resuscitation, preoxygenation, positioning, sedatives and paralytics as indicated by patient presentation. A bougie may be a helpful adjunct to successful airway placement, especially when video laryngoscopy is unavailable and the glottic opening is difficult to visualize with direct laryngoscopy. When patients cannot be oxygenated/ventilated effectively using the above interventions, or when conventional airway approaches are impossible, surgical airway management is a reasonable option.
expectedActions.rationale: The airway is now significantly more edematous because nebulized epi was never given. Plan for failure: small ETT, bougie, SGA backup, surgical cric kit visible. This is the consequence of the early miss.

expectedActions.shortAction: Start epi infusion 0.1 mcg/kg/min (90 kg × 0.1 = 9 mcg/min) once round-of-CPR epi pushes done
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Consider an epinephrine IV drip (0.5 mcg/kg/minute) when cardiovascular collapse is present despite repeated IM doses of epinephrine in conjunction with at least 60 mL/kg isotonic fluid boluses.
expectedActions.rationale: Anaphylactic arrest needs the background infusion, not just bolus epi. Start it now even while ACLS bolus epi continues.

expectedActions.shortAction: Verbalize the omission to team — teaching/QI moment for after the call
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Time to epinephrine delivery; Concentration of epinephrine in relation to route; Weight-based dosing of medications.
expectedActions.rationale: Anaphylaxis is a 'speed and dose' protocol. Calling out the timing miss in real time orients the team and creates the data point for QI later.

phases.name: Insufficient fluid resus
phases.description: JUDGMENT-ERROR BRANCH #2. The crew correctly repeats IM epi and starts the epi drip but caps fluid at a single 250 mL bolus because 'his lungs are wheezy' (misinterpreting bronchospasm as pulmonary edema). With profound vasodilation and capillary leak the tank stays empty and the patient arrests in PEA. The drip is running into a hose with no water in it. Recovery requires recognition that anaphylactic wheeze is NOT pulmonary edema, opening a second IV/IO, and giving 2-3 L wide open during the resus.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew gives IM epi repeats and starts an epi infusion but limits NS to a single 250 mL bolus due to a mistaken concern for pulmonary edema. With ongoing massive third-spacing the patient becomes more hypotensive, EtCO2 drops, and PEA arrest follows. The infusion has no volume to act on.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Stridor improved by neb epi but airway threatened by ongoing edema and now obtundation.
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Apneic. Some residual wheeze present on BVM ventilation.

skin.color: Mottled extensively; persistent urticaria; central cyanosis

otherFindings: Epi drip running but no volume to circulate
Bronchospasm was misread as pulmonary edema
Only 250 mL fluid in — protocol target is 60 mL/kg before declaring refractory
Capillary leak from anaphylaxis continues — patient is effectively dry inside

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize: anaphylaxis wheeze ≠ pulmonary edema. Give volume NOW
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If respiratory distress with wheezing is present, consider administering Albuterol 2.5–5 mg nebulized AND/OR Epinephrine 1 mg/mL, 5 mL nebulized. For signs of hypoperfusion, also administer 20 mL/kg isotonic fluid (normal saline or lactated Ringer's) rapidly (over 15 minutes) via IV or IO, and repeat as needed for ongoing hypoperfusion.
expectedActions.rationale: Bronchospasm from anaphylaxis is NOT cardiogenic pulmonary edema. The fluid-cap reflex from CHF protocols does not apply. Anaphylactic shock needs MASSIVE volume.

expectedActions.shortAction: Second large-bore IV or humeral IO immediately for rapid volume
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Establish IV access. If unable to obtain within two attempts or less than 90 seconds, place an IO needle. For IO access: The proximal humerus is the preferred site for adults.
expectedActions.rationale: One line cannot deliver volume fast enough during arrest. Humeral IO accepts pressure-bag NS at high rates.

expectedActions.shortAction: NS 2 L wide open through both access points (pressure bag/rapid infuser if available)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Hypovolemia. Additions to care include normal saline 2 L IV (or 20 mL/kg, repeated up to 3 times for pediatrics). For signs of hypoperfusion, also administer 20 mL/kg isotonic fluid (normal saline or lactated Ringer's) rapidly (over 15 minutes) via IV or IO, and repeat as needed for ongoing hypoperfusion.
expectedActions.rationale: Reversible cause for this PEA is hypovolemia from capillary leak. Run 2 L wide open immediately, plan for 4-6 L total in 90 kg patient.

expectedActions.shortAction: High-quality CPR; epi 1 mg IV q3-5 min (continue drip alongside)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Administer Epinephrine 1mg during the first or second round of compressions. Prioritize early administration of epinephrine for non-shockable rhythms.
expectedActions.rationale: Standard ACLS, but layered on the existing infusion. The drip alone wasn't enough without circulation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Secure definitive airway during CPR; bougie + 7.0 ETT, BVM with PEEP
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Advanced airway placement: Either a supraglottic airway or an endotracheal tube may be placed without interruption of compressions; Ventilations are provided at 10 breaths/minute for adults.
expectedActions.rationale: Patient still has airway edema. Tube him now while compressions continue. PEEP supports oxygenation in the residual bronchospasm.

expectedActions.shortAction: Verbalize the fluid omission to team — distinguishes bronchospasm from CHF in future
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Recognition of cardiogenic shock - If the patient condition deteriorates after fluid administration, rales or hepatomegaly develop, then consider cardiogenic shock and withholding further fluid administration.
expectedActions.rationale: The shock guideline's fluid caution applies to CARDIOGENIC shock, not anaphylactic. Naming the miss creates the teaching point.
